title: Types of number
content: Identify and use natural numbers
Identify and use integers (positive, zero and negative)
Identify and use prime numbers
Identify and use square numbers
Identify and use cube numbers
Identify and use common factors
Identify and use common multiples
Identify and use rational and irrational numbers
Identify and use reciprocals
notes_examples: Convert between numbers and words (e.g. six billion is 6000000000; 10007 is ten thousand and seven)
Express 72 as a product of its prime factors
Find the highest common factor (HCF) of two numbers
Find the lowest common multiple (LCM) of two numbers

title: Sets
content: Understand and use set language, notation and Venn diagrams to describe sets
Venn diagrams are limited to two sets
Use set notation: n(A) for number of elements in set A, A' for complement of set A, universal set, A∪B for union, A∩B for intersection
notes_examples: Example set definitions: A = {x: x is a natural number}, B = {a, b, c, …}, C = {x: a ⩽ x ⩽ b}

title: Powers and roots
content: Calculate squares and square roots
Calculate cubes and cube roots
Calculate other powers and roots of numbers
notes_examples: Recall squares and their corresponding roots from 1 to 15
Recall cubes and their corresponding roots of 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 10
Write down the value of √169
Work out 5² × ³√8

title: Fractions, decimals and percentages
content: Use the language and notation of proper fractions, improper fractions, mixed numbers, decimals and percentages in appropriate contexts
Recognise equivalence and convert between fractions, decimals and percentages
notes_examples: Candidates are expected to write fractions in their simplest form
Candidates are not expected to use recurring decimal notation
Candidates are not expected to demonstrate conversion of a recurring decimal to a fraction and vice versa

title: Ordering
content: Order quantities by magnitude
Demonstrate familiarity with the symbols =, ≠, >, <, ⩾ and ⩽

title: The four operations
content: Use the four operations for calculations with integers, fractions and decimals
Apply correct ordering of operations and use of brackets
notes_examples: Includes negative numbers
Includes improper fractions and mixed numbers
Includes practical situations such as temperature changes

title: Indices I
content: Understand and use indices (positive, zero and negative integers)
Understand and use the rules of indices
notes_examples: Find the value of 7⁻²
Find the value of 2⁻³ × 2⁴, (2³)², 2³ ÷ 2⁴

title: Standard form
content: Use the standard form A × 10ⁿ where n is a positive or negative integer and 1 ⩽ A < 10
Convert numbers into and out of standard form
Calculate with values in standard form
notes_examples: Core candidates are expected to calculate with standard form only on Paper 3

title: Estimation
content: Round values to a specified degree of accuracy
Make estimates for calculations involving numbers, quantities and measurements
Round answers to a reasonable degree of accuracy in the context of a given problem
notes_examples: Includes decimal places and significant figures
Write 5764 correct to the nearest thousand
By writing each number correct to 1 significant figure, estimate the value of 41.3 / (9.79 × 0.765)

title: Limits of accuracy
content: Give upper and lower bounds for data rounded to a specified accuracy
notes_examples: Write down the upper bound of a length measured correct to the nearest metre
Candidates are not expected to find the bounds of the results of calculations which have used data rounded to a specified accuracy

title: Ratio and proportion
content: Give ratios in their simplest form
Divide a quantity in a given ratio
Use proportional reasoning and ratios in context
notes_examples: 20:30:40 in its simplest form is 2:3:4
Adapt recipes; use map scales; determine best value

title: Rates
content: Use common measures of rate
Apply other measures of rate
Solve problems involving average speed
notes_examples: Calculate with hourly rates of pay, exchange rates between currencies, flow rates, fuel consumption
Calculate with pressure, density, population density
Required formulas will be given in the question
Knowledge of speed/distance/time formula is required
A cyclist travels 45 km in 3 hours 45 minutes – find their average speed
Notation used will be e.g. m/s (metres per second), g/cm³ (grams per cubic centimetre)

title: Percentages
content: Calculate a given percentage of a quantity
Express one quantity as a percentage of another
Calculate percentage increase or decrease
Calculate with simple and compound interest
notes_examples: Formulas are not given
Percentage calculations may include deposit, discount, profit and loss (as an amount or a percentage), earnings, percentages over 100%

title: Using a calculator
content: Use a calculator efficiently
Enter values appropriately on a calculator
Interpret the calculator display appropriately
notes_examples: Know not to round values within a calculation and to only round the final answer
Enter 2 hours 30 minutes as 2.5 hours or 2° 30' 0''
In money 4.8 means $4.80; in time 3.25 means 3 hours 15 minutes

title: Time
content: Calculate with time: seconds (s), minutes (min), hours (h), days, weeks, months, years, including the relationship between units
Calculate times in terms of the 24-hour and 12-hour clock
Read clocks and timetables
notes_examples: 1 year = 365 days
In the 24-hour clock, 3.15 a.m. is denoted by 0315 and 3.15 p.m. by 1515
Includes problems involving time zones, local times and time differences

title: Money
content: Calculate with money
Convert from one currency to another

title: Introduction to algebra
content: Know that letters can be used to represent generalised numbers
Substitute numbers into expressions and formulas

title: Algebraic manipulation
content: Simplify expressions by collecting like terms
Expand products of algebraic expressions
Factorise by extracting common factors
notes_examples: Simplify means give the answer in its simplest form (e.g. 2a + 3b + 5a – 9b = 7a – 6b)
Expand 3x(2x – 4y)
Includes products of two brackets involving one variable (e.g. expand (2x + 1)(x – 4))
Factorise means factorise fully (e.g. 9x² + 15xy = 3x(3x + 5y))

title: Indices II
content: Understand and use indices (positive, zero and negative)
Understand and use the rules of indices
notes_examples: Solve: 2ˣ = 32, find the value of x
Simplify (5x³)², 12a⁵ ÷ 3a⁻², 6x⁷y⁴ × 5x⁻⁵y
Knowledge of logarithms is not required

title: Equations
content: Construct simple expressions, equations and formulas
Solve linear equations in one unknown
Solve simultaneous linear equations in two unknowns
Change the subject of simple formulas
notes_examples: Write an expression for a number that is 2 more than n
Includes constructing linear simultaneous equations
Change the subject of formulas where the subject only appears once and there is not a power or root of the subject

title: Inequalities
content: Represent and interpret inequalities, including on a number line
notes_examples: Open circles represent strict inequalities (< and >)
Closed circles represent inclusive inequalities (⩽ and ⩾)
Example: –3 ⩽ x < 1 shown on a number line

title: Sequences
content: Continue a given number sequence or pattern
Recognise patterns in sequences, including the term-to-term rule and relationships between different sequences
Find and use the nth term of linear sequences
Find and use the nth term of simple quadratic sequences
Find and use the nth term of simple cubic sequences
notes_examples: Write the next two terms in the sequence: 1, 3, 6, 10, 15, …
Find the nth term of 2, 5, 10, 17

title: Graphs in practical situations
content: Use and interpret graphs in practical situations including travel graphs and conversion graphs
Draw graphs from given data
notes_examples: Interpret the gradient of a straight-line graph as a rate of change
Draw a distance–time graph to represent a journey

title: Graphs of functions
content: Construct tables of values, and draw, recognise and interpret graphs for functions of the forms: ax + b, ±x² + ax + b, a/x (x ≠ 0), where a and b are integer constants
Solve associated equations graphically, including finding and interpreting roots by graphical methods
notes_examples: Find the intersection of a line and a curve

title: Sketching curves
content: Recognise, sketch and interpret graphs of linear functions
Recognise, sketch and interpret graphs of quadratic functions
notes_examples: Knowledge of symmetry and roots is required for quadratic functions
Knowledge of turning points is not required

title: Coordinates
content: Use and interpret Cartesian coordinates in two dimensions

title: Drawing linear graphs
content: Draw straight-line graphs for linear equations
notes_examples: Equations will be given in the form y = mx + c (e.g. y = –2x + 5), unless a table of values is given

title: Gradient of linear graphs
content: Find the gradient of a straight line
notes_examples: From a grid only

title: Equations of linear graphs
content: Interpret and obtain the equation of a straight-line graph in the form y = mx + c
notes_examples: Questions may use and request lines in the forms y = mx + c or x = k
May involve finding the equation when the graph is given
May ask for the gradient or y-intercept from an equation (e.g. find the gradient and y-intercept of y = 6x + 3)
Candidates are expected to give equations of a line in a fully simplified form

title: Parallel lines
content: Find the gradient and equation of a straight line parallel to a given line
notes_examples: Find the equation of the line parallel to y = 4x – 1 that passes through (1, –3)

title: Geometrical terms
content: Use and interpret geometrical terms: point, vertex, line, parallel, perpendicular, bearing, right angle, acute/obtuse/reflex angles, interior and exterior angles, similar, congruent, scale factor
Use and interpret vocabulary of triangles, special quadrilaterals, polygons, nets and simple solids
Use and interpret vocabulary of a circle
notes_examples: Candidates are not expected to show that two shapes are congruent
Triangles: equilateral, isosceles, scalene, right-angled
Quadrilaterals: square, rectangle, kite, rhombus, parallelogram, trapezium
Polygons: regular and irregular polygons, pentagon, hexagon, octagon, decagon
Simple solids: cube, cuboid, prism, cylinder, pyramid, cone, sphere (hemisphere not required), face, surface, edge
Circle vocabulary: centre, radius, diameter, circumference, semicircle, chord, tangent, arc, sector, segment

title: Geometrical constructions
content: Measure and draw lines and angles
Construct a triangle, given the lengths of all sides, using a ruler and pair of compasses only
Draw, use and interpret nets
notes_examples: A ruler should be used for all straight edges
Constructions of perpendicular bisectors and angle bisectors are not required
Construct a rhombus by drawing two triangles; construction arcs must be shown
Draw nets of cubes, cuboids, prisms and pyramids
Use measurements from nets to calculate volumes and surface areas

title: Scale drawings
content: Draw and interpret scale drawings
Use and interpret three-figure bearings
notes_examples: A ruler must be used for all straight edges
Bearings are measured clockwise from north (000° to 360°)
Find the bearing of A from B if the bearing of B from A is 025°
Includes understanding of north, east, south and west (e.g. point D is due east of point C)

title: Similarity
content: Calculate lengths of similar shapes

title: Symmetry
content: Recognise line symmetry and order of rotational symmetry in two dimensions
notes_examples: Includes properties of triangles, quadrilaterals and polygons directly related to their symmetries

title: Angles
content: Calculate unknown angles using: sum of angles at a point = 360°, sum of angles on a straight line = 180°, vertically opposite angles are equal, angle sum of triangle = 180° and quadrilateral = 360°
Calculate unknown angles for angles in parallel lines: corresponding angles are equal, alternate angles are equal, co-interior angles sum to 180°
Know and use angle properties of regular polygons
notes_examples: Knowledge of three-letter notation for angles is required (e.g. angle ABC)
Candidates are expected to use correct geometrical terminology when giving reasons
Includes exterior and interior angles and angle sum

title: Circle theorems
content: Calculate unknown angles using: angle in a semicircle = 90°, angle between tangent and radius = 90°
notes_examples: Candidates will be expected to use the geometrical properties listed in the syllabus when giving reasons for answers

title: Units of measure
content: Use metric units of mass, length, area, volume and capacity in practical situations
Convert quantities into larger or smaller units
notes_examples: Units include: mm, cm, m, km; mm², cm², m², km²; mm³, cm³, m³; ml, l; g, kg
Conversion includes between different units of area (e.g. cm² ↔ m²) and between units of volume and capacity (e.g. m³ ↔ litres)

title: Area and perimeter
content: Carry out calculations involving the perimeter and area of a rectangle, triangle, parallelogram and trapezium
notes_examples: Except for area of a triangle, formulas are not given

title: Circles, arcs and sectors
content: Carry out calculations involving the circumference and area of a circle
Carry out calculations involving arc length and sector area as fractions of the circumference and area of a circle, where the sector angle is a factor of 360°
notes_examples: Answers may be asked for in terms of π
Formulas are given in the List of formulas

title: Surface area and volume
content: Carry out calculations and solve problems involving the surface area and volume of a cuboid, prism, cylinder, sphere, pyramid and cone
notes_examples: Answers may be asked for in terms of π

Formulas given in the List of formulas: curved surface area of a cylinder, curved surface area of a cone, surface area of a sphere, volume of a prism, volume of a pyramid, volume of a cylinder, volume of a cone, volume of a sphere
The term prism refers to any solid with a uniform cross-section (e.g. a cylindrical sector)

title: Compound shapes and parts of shapes
content: Carry out calculations and solve problems involving perimeters and areas of compound shapes and parts of shapes
Carry out calculations and solve problems involving surface areas and volumes of compound solids and parts of solids
notes_examples: Answers may be asked for in terms of π
Find the volume of half of a sphere

title: Pythagoras' theorem
content: Know and use Pythagoras' theorem

title: Right-angled triangles
content: Know and use the sine, cosine and tangent ratios for acute angles in calculations involving sides and angles of a right-angled triangle
Solve problems in two dimensions using Pythagoras' theorem and trigonometry
notes_examples: Angles will be given in degrees and answers should be written in degrees, with decimals correct to one decimal place
Knowledge of bearings may be required

title: Transformations
content: Recognise, describe and draw reflection of a shape in a vertical or horizontal line
Recognise, describe and draw rotation of a shape about the origin, vertices or midpoints of edges through multiples of 90°
Recognise, describe and draw enlargement of a shape from a centre by a scale factor
Recognise, describe and draw translation of a shape by a vector
notes_examples: Questions will not involve combinations of transformations
A ruler must be used for all straight edges
Positive and fractional scale factors only

title: Introduction to probability
content: Understand and use the probability scale from 0 to 1
Calculate the probability of a single event
Understand that the probability of an event not occurring = 1 – the probability of the event occurring
notes_examples: Probability notation is not required
Probabilities should be given as a fraction, decimal or percentage
Problems may require using information from tables, graphs or Venn diagrams (limited to two sets)
The probability that a counter is blue is 0.8 – find the probability that it is not blue

title: Relative and expected frequencies
content: Understand relative frequency as an estimate of probability
Calculate expected frequencies
notes_examples: Use results of experiments with a spinner to estimate the probability of a given outcome
Use probability to estimate an expected value from a population
Includes understanding what is meant by fair, bias and random

title: Probability of combined events
content: Calculate the probability of combined events using sample space diagrams, Venn diagrams and tree diagrams where appropriate
notes_examples: Combined events will only be with replacement
Venn diagrams will be limited to two sets
In tree diagrams, outcomes will be written at the end of branches and probabilities by the side of the branches

title: Classifying statistical data
content: Classify and tabulate statistical data
notes_examples: Tally tables, two-way tables

title: Interpreting statistical data
content: Read, interpret and draw inferences from tables and statistical diagrams
Compare sets of data using tables, graphs and statistical measures
Appreciate restrictions on drawing conclusions from given data
notes_examples: Compare averages and ranges between two data sets

title: Averages and range
content: Calculate the mean, median, mode and range for individual data
Distinguish between the purposes for which mean, median, mode and range are used
notes_examples: Data may be in a list or frequency table, but will not be grouped

title: Statistical charts and diagrams
content: Draw and interpret bar charts
Draw and interpret pie charts
Draw and interpret pictograms
Draw and interpret stem-and-leaf diagrams
Draw and interpret simple frequency distributions
notes_examples: Includes composite (stacked) and dual (side-by-side) bar charts
Stem-and-leaf diagrams should have ordered data with a key

title: Scatter diagrams
content: Draw and interpret scatter diagrams
Understand positive, negative and zero correlation
Draw by eye, interpret and use a straight line of best fit
notes_examples: Plotted points should be clearly marked as small crosses (×)
A line of best fit should be a single ruled line drawn by inspection
The line of best fit should extend across the full data set
The line does not need to coincide exactly with any points but there should be a roughly even distribution of points either side

title: Types of number
content: Identify and use natural numbers
Identify and use integers (positive, zero and negative)
Identify and use prime numbers
Identify and use square numbers
Identify and use cube numbers
Identify and use common factors
Identify and use common multiples
Identify and use rational and irrational numbers
Identify and use reciprocals
notes_examples: Convert between numbers and words (e.g. six billion is 6000000000; 10007 is ten thousand and seven)
Express 72 as a product of its prime factors
Find the highest common factor (HCF) of two numbers
Find the lowest common multiple (LCM) of two numbers

title: Sets
content: Understand and use set language, notation and Venn diagrams to describe sets and represent relationships between sets
Venn diagrams are limited to two or three sets
Use set notation: n(A), ∈, ∉, A', ∅, universal set, A⊆B, A⊈B, A∪B, A∩B
notes_examples: Example set definitions: A = {x: x is a natural number}, B = {(x, y): y = mx + c}, C = {x: a ⩽ x ⩽ b}, D = {a, b, c, …}

title: Powers and roots
content: Calculate squares and square roots
Calculate cubes and cube roots
Calculate other powers and roots of numbers
notes_examples: Recall squares and their corresponding roots from 1 to 15
Recall cubes and their corresponding roots of 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 10
Write down the value of √169
Work out 5² × ³√8

title: Fractions, decimals and percentages
content: Use the language and notation of proper fractions, improper fractions, mixed numbers, decimals and percentages in appropriate contexts
Recognise equivalence and convert between fractions, decimals and percentages
Use and understand recurring decimal notation
Convert between recurring decimals and fractions
notes_examples: Candidates are expected to write fractions in their simplest form
Recurring decimal notation is required: e.g. 0.1̄7 = 0.1777…, 0.1̄2̄3̄ = 0.123123…
Convert recurring decimals to fractions (e.g. write 0.1̄7 as a fraction)

title: Ordering
content: Order quantities by magnitude
Demonstrate familiarity with the symbols =, ≠, >, <, ⩾ and ⩽

title: The four operations
content: Use the four operations for calculations with integers, fractions and decimals
Apply correct ordering of operations and use of brackets
notes_examples: Includes negative numbers, improper fractions, mixed numbers
Includes practical situations such as temperature changes

title: Indices I
content: Understand and use indices (positive, zero, negative and fractional)
Understand and use the rules of indices
notes_examples: Find the value of 7⁻², 81^(1/2), 8^(–2/3)
Find the value of 2⁻³ × 2⁴, (2³)², 2³ ÷ 2⁴

title: Standard form
content: Use the standard form A × 10ⁿ where n is a positive or negative integer and 1 ⩽ A < 10
Convert numbers into and out of standard form
Calculate with values in standard form

title: Estimation
content: Round values to a specified degree of accuracy
Make estimates for calculations involving numbers, quantities and measurements
Round answers to a reasonable degree of accuracy in the context of a given problem
notes_examples: Includes decimal places and significant figures
Write 5764 correct to the nearest thousand
By writing each number correct to 1 significant figure, estimate the value of 41.3 / (9.79 × 0.765)

title: Limits of accuracy
content: Give upper and lower bounds for data rounded to a specified accuracy
Find upper and lower bounds of the results of calculations which have used data rounded to a specified accuracy
notes_examples: Write down the upper bound of a length measured correct to the nearest metre
Calculate the upper bound of the perimeter or area of a rectangle given dimensions measured to the nearest centimetre
Find the lower bound of the speed given rounded values of distance and time

title: Ratio and proportion
content: Give ratios in their simplest form
Divide a quantity in a given ratio
Use proportional reasoning and ratios in context
notes_examples: 20:30:40 in its simplest form is 2:3:4
Adapt recipes; use map scales; determine best value

title: Rates
content: Use common measures of rate
Apply other measures of rate
Solve problems involving average speed
notes_examples: Calculate with hourly rates of pay, exchange rates between currencies, flow rates, fuel consumption
Calculate with pressure, density, population density
Required formulas will be given in the question
Knowledge of speed/distance/time formula is required
A cyclist travels 45 km in 3 hours 45 minutes – find their average speed
Notation used: m/s (metres per second), g/cm³ (grams per cubic centimetre)

title: Percentages
content: Calculate a given percentage of a quantity
Express one quantity as a percentage of another
Calculate percentage increase or decrease
Calculate with simple and compound interest
Calculate using reverse percentages
notes_examples: Problems may include repeated percentage change
Formulas are not given
Find the cost price given the selling price and the percentage profit
Percentage calculations may include: deposit, discount, profit and loss, earnings, percentages over 100%

title: Using a calculator
content: Use a calculator efficiently
Enter values appropriately on a calculator
Interpret the calculator display appropriately
notes_examples: Know not to round values within a calculation and to only round the final answer
Enter 2 hours 30 minutes as 2.5 hours or 2° 30' 0''
In money 4.8 means $4.80; in time 3.25 means 3 hours 15 minutes

title: Time
content: Calculate with time: seconds (s), minutes (min), hours (h), days, weeks, months, years, including the relationship between units
Calculate times in terms of the 24-hour and 12-hour clock
Read clocks and timetables
notes_examples: 1 year = 365 days
In the 24-hour clock, 3.15 a.m. is denoted by 0315 and 3.15 p.m. by 1515
Includes problems involving time zones, local times and time differences

title: Money
content: Calculate with money
Convert from one currency to another

title: Exponential growth and decay
content: Use exponential growth and decay
notes_examples: Examples: depreciation, population change
Knowledge of e is not required

title: Surds
content: Understand and use surds, including simplifying expressions
Rationalise the denominator

title: Introduction to algebra
content: Know that letters can be used to represent generalised numbers
Substitute numbers into expressions and formulas

title: Algebraic manipulation
content: Simplify expressions by collecting like terms
Expand products of algebraic expressions
Factorise by extracting common factors
Factorise expressions of the form: ax + bx + kay + kby, a²x² – b²y², a² + 2ab + b², ax² + bx + c, ax³ + bx² + cx
Complete the square for expressions in the form ax² + bx + c
notes_examples: Simplify: 2a² + 3ab – 1 + 5a² – 9ab + 4 = 7a² – 6ab + 3
Expand 3x(2x – 4y), (3x + y)(x – 4y)
Includes products of more than two brackets (e.g. expand (x – 2)(x + 3)(2x + 1))
Factorise fully (e.g. 9x² + 15xy = 3x(3x + 5y))

title: Algebraic fractions
content: Manipulate algebraic fractions
Factorise and simplify rational expressions
notes_examples: Simplify (x² – 2x)/(x² – 5x + 6)

title: Indices II
content: Understand and use indices (positive, zero, negative and fractional)
Understand and use the rules of indices
notes_examples: Knowledge of logarithms is not required

title: Equations
content: Construct expressions, equations and formulas
Solve linear equations in one unknown
Solve fractional equations with numerical and linear algebraic denominators
Solve simultaneous linear equations in two unknowns
Solve simultaneous equations involving one linear and one non-linear
Solve quadratic equations by factorisation, completing the square and by use of the quadratic formula
Change the subject of formulas
notes_examples: Write an expression for the product of two consecutive even numbers
Includes constructing simultaneous equations
Solve fractional equations: x/(2x + 1) = 4; 2/(x + 2) + 3/(2x – 1) = 1; x/(x + 2) = 3/(x – 6)
Simultaneous non-linear: powers no higher than two
Includes writing a quadratic expression in completed square form
Candidates may be expected to give solutions in surd form
The quadratic formula is given in the List of formulas
Change subject where the subject appears twice or where there is a power or root of the subject

title: Inequalities
content: Represent and interpret inequalities, including on a number line
Construct, solve and interpret linear inequalities
Represent and interpret linear inequalities in two variables graphically
List inequalities that define a given region
notes_examples: Open circles for strict inequalities (< and >); closed circles for inclusive inequalities (⩽ and ⩾)
Broken lines for strict inequalities; solid lines for inclusive inequalities
Shading used to represent unwanted regions (unless otherwise directed)
Linear programming problems are not included

title: Sequences
content: Continue a given number sequence or pattern
Recognise patterns in sequences, including the term-to-term rule and relationships between different sequences
Find and use the nth term of sequences
notes_examples: Subscript notation may be used (e.g. Tₙ is the nth term of sequence T)
Includes linear, quadratic, cubic and exponential sequences and simple combinations of these

title: Proportion
content: Express direct and inverse proportion in algebraic terms
Use algebraic expressions of proportion to find unknown quantities
notes_examples: Includes linear, square, square root, cube and cube root proportion
Knowledge of proportional symbol (∝) is required

title: Graphs in practical situations
content: Use and interpret graphs in practical situations including travel graphs and conversion graphs
Draw graphs from given data
Apply the idea of rate of change to simple kinematics involving distance–time and speed–time graphs, acceleration and deceleration
Calculate distance travelled as area under a speed–time graph
notes_examples: Includes estimation and interpretation of the gradient of a tangent at a point
Areas will involve linear sections of the graph only

title: Graphs of functions
content: Construct tables of values, and draw, recognise and interpret graphs for functions of the forms: axⁿ (sums of no more than three of these) and ab^x + c, where n = –2, –1, –1/2, 0, 1/2, 1, 2, 3; a and c are rational numbers; b is a positive integer
Solve associated equations graphically, including finding and interpreting roots by graphical methods
Draw and interpret graphs representing exponential growth and decay problems
notes_examples: y = x³ + x – 4
y = 2x + 3/x²
Find the intersection of a line and a curve

title: Sketching curves
content: Recognise, sketch and interpret graphs of linear functions
Recognise, sketch and interpret graphs of quadratic functions
Recognise, sketch and interpret graphs of cubic functions
Recognise, sketch and interpret graphs of reciprocal functions
Recognise, sketch and interpret graphs of exponential functions
notes_examples: Functions equivalent to: ax + by = c, y = ax² + bx + c, y = ax³ + b, y = ax³ + bx² + cx, y = a/x + b, y = arˣ + b (a, b, c rational; r rational positive)
Knowledge of turning points, roots and symmetry is required
Knowledge of vertical and horizontal asymptotes is required
Finding turning points of quadratics by completing the square is required

title: Differentiation
content: Estimate gradients of curves by drawing tangents
Use the derivatives of functions of the form axⁿ where a is a rational constant and n is a positive integer or zero, and simple sums of not more than three of these
Apply differentiation to gradients and stationary points (turning points)
Discriminate between maxima and minima by any method
notes_examples: dy/dx notation will be expected
Maximum and minimum points may be identified by: an accurate sketch, use of the second differential, or inspecting the gradient either side of a turning point
Candidates are not expected to identify points of inflection

title: Functions
content: Understand functions, domain and range and use function notation
Understand and find inverse functions f⁻¹(x)
Form composite functions as defined by gf(x) = g(f(x))
notes_examples: Given f(x) = 3/(x + 2) and g(x) = (3x + 5)², find fg(x) as a fraction in its simplest form
Candidates are not expected to find the domains and ranges of composite functions
This topic may include mapping diagrams

title: Coordinates
content: Use and interpret Cartesian coordinates in two dimensions

title: Drawing linear graphs
content: Draw straight-line graphs for linear equations

title: Gradient of linear graphs
content: Find the gradient of a straight line
Calculate the gradient of a straight line from the coordinates of two points on it

title: Length and midpoint
content: Calculate the length of a line segment
Find the coordinates of the midpoint of a line segment

title: Equations of linear graphs
content: Interpret and obtain the equation of a straight-line graph
notes_examples: Questions may use and request lines in forms: ax + by = c, y = mx + c, x = k
May involve finding the equation when the graph is given
May ask for gradient or y-intercept from an equation (e.g. find gradient and y-intercept of 5x + 4y = 8)
Candidates are expected to give equations in a fully simplified form

title: Parallel lines
content: Find the gradient and equation of a straight line parallel to a given line
notes_examples: Find the equation of the line parallel to y = 4x – 1 that passes through (1, –3)

title: Perpendicular lines
content: Find the gradient and equation of a straight line perpendicular to a given line
notes_examples: Find the gradient of a line perpendicular to 2y = 3x + 1
Find the equation of the perpendicular bisector of the line joining the points (–3, 8) and (9, –2)

title: Geometrical terms
content: Use and interpret geometrical terms: point, vertex, line, plane, parallel, perpendicular, perpendicular bisector, bearing, right angle, acute/obtuse/reflex angles, interior and exterior angles, similar, congruent, scale factor
Use and interpret vocabulary of triangles, special quadrilaterals, polygons, nets and solids
Use and interpret vocabulary of a circle
notes_examples: Candidates are not expected to show that two shapes are congruent
Triangles: equilateral, isosceles, scalene, right-angled
Quadrilaterals: square, rectangle, kite, rhombus, parallelogram, trapezium
Polygons: regular and irregular polygons, pentagon, hexagon, octagon, decagon
Solids: cube, cuboid, prism, cylinder, pyramid, cone, sphere, hemisphere, frustum, face, surface, edge
Circle vocabulary: centre, radius, diameter, circumference, semicircle, chord, tangent, major and minor arc, sector, segment

title: Geometrical constructions
content: Measure and draw lines and angles
Construct a triangle given the lengths of all sides using a ruler and pair of compasses only
Draw, use and interpret nets
notes_examples: A ruler should be used for all straight edges
Constructions of perpendicular bisectors and angle bisectors are not required
Construct a rhombus by drawing two triangles; construction arcs must be shown
Draw nets of cubes, cuboids, prisms and pyramids
Use measurements from nets to calculate volumes and surface areas

title: Scale drawings
content: Draw and interpret scale drawings
Use and interpret three-figure bearings
notes_examples: A ruler must be used for all straight edges
Bearings are measured clockwise from north (000° to 360°)
Find the bearing of A from B if the bearing of B from A is 025°
Includes understanding of north, east, south and west (e.g. point D is due east of point C)

title: Similarity
content: Calculate lengths of similar shapes
Use the relationships between lengths and areas of similar shapes and lengths, surface areas and volumes of similar solids
Solve problems and give simple explanations involving similarity
notes_examples: Includes use of scale factor: Volume of A / Volume of B = (Length of A / Length of B)³
Includes showing that two triangles are similar using geometric reasons

title: Symmetry
content: Recognise line symmetry and order of rotational symmetry in two dimensions
Recognise symmetry properties of prisms, cylinders, pyramids and cones
notes_examples: Includes properties of triangles, quadrilaterals and polygons directly related to their symmetries
Identify planes and axes of symmetry

title: Angles
content: Calculate unknown angles using: sum of angles at a point = 360°, sum of angles on a straight line = 180°, vertically opposite angles are equal, angle sum of triangle = 180° and quadrilateral = 360°
Calculate unknown angles for angles in parallel lines: corresponding angles are equal, alternate angles are equal, co-interior angles sum to 180°
Know and use angle properties of regular and irregular polygons
notes_examples: Knowledge of 3-letter notation for angles is required (e.g. angle ABC)
Candidates are expected to use correct geometrical terminology when giving reasons
Includes exterior and interior angles and angle sum

title: Circle theorems I
content: Calculate unknown angles using: angle in a semicircle = 90°, angle between tangent and radius = 90°, angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference, angles in the same segment are equal, opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°, alternate segment theorem
notes_examples: Candidates are expected to use the geometrical properties listed in the syllabus when giving reasons for answers

title: Circle theorems II
content: Use symmetry properties of circles: equal chords are equidistant from the centre, the perpendicular bisector of a chord passes through the centre, tangents from an external point are equal in length
notes_examples: Candidates are expected to use the geometrical properties listed in the syllabus when giving reasons for answers

title: Units of measure
content: Use metric units of mass, length, area, volume and capacity in practical situations
Convert quantities into larger or smaller units
notes_examples: Units include: mm, cm, m, km; mm², cm², m², km²; mm³, cm³, m³; ml, l; g, kg
Conversion includes between different units of area (e.g. cm² ↔ m²) and between units of volume and capacity (e.g. m³ ↔ litres)

title: Area and perimeter
content: Carry out calculations involving the perimeter and area of a rectangle, triangle, parallelogram and trapezium
notes_examples: Except for the area of a triangle, formulas are not given

title: Circles, arcs and sectors
content: Carry out calculations involving the circumference and area of a circle
Carry out calculations involving arc length and sector area as fractions of the circumference and area of a circle
notes_examples: Answers may be asked for in terms of π
Formulas are given in the List of formulas
Includes minor and major sectors

title: Surface area and volume
content: Carry out calculations and solve problems involving the surface area and volume of a cuboid, prism, cylinder, sphere, pyramid and cone
notes_examples: Answers may be asked for in terms of π

Formulas given in the List of formulas: curved surface area of a cylinder, curved surface area of a cone, surface area of a sphere, volume of a prism, volume of a pyramid, volume of a cylinder, volume of a cone, volume of a sphere
The term prism refers to any solid with a uniform cross-section (e.g. a cylindrical sector)

title: Compound shapes and parts of shapes
content: Carry out calculations and solve problems involving perimeters and areas of compound shapes and parts of shapes
Carry out calculations and solve problems involving surface areas and volumes of compound solids and parts of solids
notes_examples: Answers may be asked for in terms of π
Find the surface area and volume of a frustum

title: Pythagoras' theorem
content: Know and use Pythagoras' theorem

title: Right-angled triangles
content: Know and use the sine, cosine and tangent ratios for acute angles in calculations involving sides and angles of a right-angled triangle
Solve problems in two dimensions using Pythagoras' theorem and trigonometry
Know that the perpendicular distance from a point to a line is the shortest distance to the line
Carry out calculations involving angles of elevation and depression
notes_examples: Angles will be given in degrees and answers should be written in degrees, with decimals correct to one decimal place
Knowledge of bearings may be required

title: Exact trigonometric values
content: Know the exact values of sin x and cos x for x = 0°, 30°, 45°, 60° and 90°
Know the exact values of tan x for x = 0°, 30°, 45° and 60°

title: Trigonometric functions
content: Recognise, sketch and interpret graphs of y = sin x, y = cos x and y = tan x for 0° ⩽ x ⩽ 360°
Solve trigonometric equations involving sin x, cos x or tan x for 0° ⩽ x ⩽ 360°
notes_examples: Solve: sin x = √3/2 for 0° ⩽ x ⩽ 360°
Solve: 2 cos x + 1 = 0 for 0° ⩽ x ⩽ 360°

title: Non-right-angled triangles
content: Use the sine and cosine rules in calculations involving lengths and angles for any triangle
Use the formula: area of triangle = (1/2)ab sin C
notes_examples: Includes problems involving obtuse angles and the ambiguous case
The sine and cosine rules and the formula for area of a triangle are given in the List of formulas

title: Pythagoras' theorem and trigonometry in 3D
content: Carry out calculations and solve problems in three dimensions using Pythagoras' theorem and trigonometry, including calculating the angle between a line and a plane

title: Transformations
content: Recognise, describe and draw reflection of a shape in a straight line
Recognise, describe and draw rotation of a shape about a centre through multiples of 90°
Recognise, describe and draw enlargement of a shape from a centre by a scale factor
Recognise, describe and draw translation of a shape by a vector
notes_examples: Questions may involve combinations of transformations
A ruler must be used for all straight edges
Positive, fractional and negative scale factors may be used

title: Vectors in two dimensions
content: Describe a translation using a vector represented by column vector, AB or a
Add and subtract vectors
Multiply a vector by a scalar
notes_examples: Vectors will be printed as AB (with arrow) or bold a

title: Magnitude of a vector
content: Calculate the magnitude of a vector (x, y) as √(x² + y²)
notes_examples: The magnitudes of vectors will be denoted by modulus signs (e.g. |a| is the magnitude of a, |AB| is the magnitude of AB)

title: Vector geometry
content: Represent vectors by directed line segments
Use position vectors
Use the sum and difference of two or more vectors to express given vectors in terms of two coplanar vectors
Use vectors to reason and to solve geometric problems
notes_examples: Show that vectors are parallel
Show that 3 points are collinear
Solve vector problems involving ratio and similarity

title: Introduction to probability
content: Understand and use the probability scale from 0 to 1
Understand and use probability notation
Calculate the probability of a single event
Understand that the probability of an event not occurring = 1 – the probability of the event occurring
notes_examples: P(A) is the probability of A; P(A') is the probability of not A
Probabilities should be given as a fraction, decimal or percentage
Problems may require using information from tables, graphs or Venn diagrams
Given P(B) = 0.8, find P(B')

title: Relative and expected frequencies
content: Understand relative frequency as an estimate of probability
Calculate expected frequencies
notes_examples: Use results of experiments with a spinner to estimate the probability of a given outcome
Use probability to estimate an expected value from a population
Includes understanding what is meant by fair, bias and random

title: Probability of combined events
content: Calculate the probability of combined events using sample space diagrams, Venn diagrams and tree diagrams where appropriate
notes_examples: Combined events could be with or without replacement
The notation P(A ∩ B) and P(A ∪ B) may be used in the context of Venn diagrams
On tree diagrams, outcomes written at end of branches and probabilities by the side of branches

title: Conditional probability
content: Calculate conditional probability using Venn diagrams, tree diagrams and tables
notes_examples: Knowledge of notation P(A|B) and formulas relating to conditional probability is not required

title: Classifying statistical data
content: Classify and tabulate statistical data
notes_examples: Tally tables, two-way tables

title: Interpreting statistical data
content: Read, interpret and draw inferences from tables and statistical diagrams
Compare sets of data using tables, graphs and statistical measures
Appreciate restrictions on drawing conclusions from given data
notes_examples: Compare averages and measures of spread between two data sets

title: Averages and measures of spread
content: Calculate the mean, median, mode, quartiles, range and interquartile range for individual data and distinguish between the purposes for which these are used
Calculate an estimate of the mean for grouped discrete or grouped continuous data
Identify the modal class from a grouped frequency distribution

title: Statistical charts and diagrams
content: Draw and interpret bar charts
Draw and interpret pie charts
Draw and interpret pictograms
Draw and interpret stem-and-leaf diagrams
Draw and interpret simple frequency distributions
notes_examples: Includes composite (stacked) and dual (side-by-side) bar charts
Stem-and-leaf diagrams should have ordered data with a key

title: Scatter diagrams
content: Draw and interpret scatter diagrams
Understand positive, negative and zero correlation
Draw by eye, interpret and use a straight line of best fit
notes_examples: Plotted points should be clearly marked as small crosses (×)
A line of best fit should be a single ruled line drawn by inspection
The line should extend across the full data set
The line does not need to coincide exactly with any points but there should be a roughly even distribution of points either side

title: Cumulative frequency diagrams
content: Draw and interpret cumulative frequency tables and diagrams
Estimate and interpret the median, percentiles, quartiles and interquartile range from cumulative frequency diagrams
notes_examples: Plotted points on a cumulative frequency diagram should be clearly marked as small crosses (×) and joined with a smooth curve

title: Histograms
content: Draw and interpret histograms
Calculate with frequency density
notes_examples: On histograms, the vertical axis is labelled 'Frequency density'
Frequency density = frequency ÷ class width
